session_hint: the Kubernetes pod DNS resolution timeout investigation
question: What was causing intermittent DNS resolution timeouts in Kubernetes pods?
turns: Pods in our data-processing namespace are experiencing 2-3 second DNS resolution delays for external hostnames like api.stripe.com and s3.amazonaws.com. About 15% of lookups are affected. First suspect: CoreDNS pods are under-resourced and getting OOMKilled.

Checked CoreDNS pods: 0 restarts in 7 days, memory at 45Mi against a 170Mi limit, CPU at 3m against 100m limit. CoreDNS metrics show 99th percentile latency of 4ms for cache hits. CoreDNS itself is healthy. Ruling out CoreDNS resource exhaustion.

Second suspect: the upstream resolver (8.8.8.8 configured in CoreDNS Corefile) is intermittently slow. SSHed to the node and ran `dig @8.8.8.8 api.stripe.com` 50 times — all resolved in under 30ms, zero failures. Upstream resolver is fine. Ruling that out.

Inspected /etc/resolv.conf inside a pod: ndots:5 is set (Kubernetes default). For api.stripe.com (2 dots, below ndots threshold of 5), the resolver first tries api.stripe.com.data-processing.svc.cluster.local, then api.stripe.com.svc.cluster.local, then api.stripe.com.cluster.local, then api.stripe.com.eu-west-1.compute.internal — all failing with NXDOMAIN (500ms timeout each) — before finally attempting the absolute name. That's up to 2.5 seconds of failed search domain lookups. Fixed by adding ndots:1 in the pod's dnsConfig. External FQDNs now resolve in under 30ms. Root cause: ndots:5 default causing multiple failed search domain lookups before resolving external names.
